body_txt: Let's say a storyline requires a character who is unappealing, unattractive , and has a hard time finding dates. However, actual plain-looking actors ( especially women ) almost never find work in Hollywood — producers know that "beauty is money", after all, and are therefore disinclined to cast genuinely unattractive people even as villains or extras — so this "homely" character will very often be played by someone more gorgeous than most people you'd meet in real life, with said homeliness mostly just implied via in-universe reactions. Characters are made "plain" by giving them thick glasses , braces , freckles , unfashionable clothes, and an unflattering hairstyle, and surrounding them with people who are more attractive. A subtle method used is to give the actor clothes that clash with their natural skin color, making them look pale or blotchy, a method also used in " before-and-after " shots for diet-pill commercials. Bad lighting is also a good trick. The character may also be a Sickly Neurotic Geek . A variation of this can also happen in illustrated storytelling where there are no actors appearing physically, such as Animation , or no actors at all, such as Comic Books . In that case, the reasons are more often because the artists either are unable to draw genuinelly unattractive people (particularly women) or because the artist or entire artistic styles are limited in how variable the characters are, resulting in Only Six Faces . Note: Just because a character is bullied by an Alpha Bitch , it doesn't necessarily mean they're considered ugly In-Universe . As in real life, fictional bullies often target attractive rivals because they're insecure and envious of that person's good looks , or simply opportunistically go after whoever can't fight back against them. Likewise, a character can be oblivious to their own good looks and have a poor self-image without being considered Hollywood Homely. Since beauty is very subjective in real-life, this trope only counts if the character is considered unattractive by a majority of other characters In-Universe . See also : Adaptational Attractiveness , Beauty Inversion , I'm Not Pretty , Beautiful All Along , Unconvincingly Unpopular Character , "All Grown Up" Remark , Formerly Fat , Hidden Buxom , Hollywood Pudgy , and She Cleans Up Nicely . Generic Cuteness is the animated equivalent of this trope, and the result is a very Informed Deformity . Contrast Informed Attractiveness and Gorgeous Gorgon . Tends to go hand-in-hand with the Unnecessary Makeover , with an attempt to undo this resulting in a character looking less attractive.
